content:
Understanding the Real Difference Between Secured and Unsecured Business Loans

When arranging business loans in the UK, one of the first questions is whether the facility should be secured or unsecured.

A lot of directors instinctively lean toward unsecured funding because it sounds safer.

No assets tied up.
No charges registered.

But that’s not always how it works in practice.

The better question is:

What are you actually trying to achieve — and over what time frame?

The answer usually determines the right structure far more than preference does.

What Is a Secured Business Loan?

A secured business loan means the lender takes security over an asset. That might be:

  • Commercial property
  • Investment property
  • Land
  • Equipment
  • Debtor books
  • Occasionally residential property (with advice)

Because there’s security in place, the lender’s risk reduces. And when risk reduces, pricing and terms often improve.

That’s why secured lending is common within:

  • Commercial Mortgages
  • Development Finance
  • Bridging Finance
  • Larger structured business loans

If the funding is long-term or asset-backed, secured facilities usually make commercial sense.

That doesn’t mean they’re automatically better. It just means they’re often more efficient when the circumstances are right.

What Is an Unsecured Business Loan?

An unsecured business loan doesn’t involve a property charge.

Instead, approval tends to rely on:

  • Trading performance
  • Profit levels
  • Cash flow
  • Credit history
  • Bank conduct

These facilities are typically faster to arrange and involve less legal process.

They’re commonly used for:

  • Working capital
  • VAT or tax timing gaps
  • Stock purchases
  • Marketing investment
  • Short-term cash flow pressure

They do, however, come with higher pricing and shorter repayment periods in most cases.

Secured vs Unsecured: Key Structural Differences

Factor Secured Loan Unsecured Loan
Asset Required Yes No (usually)
Typical Rates Lower Higher
Loan Size Larger Smaller
Term Length Longer Short to medium
Speed Moderate Often faster
Flexibility Depends on lender Can be rigid

Where Directors Sometimes Get It Wrong

“Unsecured” doesn’t automatically mean low risk.

Many unsecured facilities still require:

  • Personal guarantees
  • Debentures
  • Cross-director guarantees

So while no property charge is registered, exposure may still sit with the directors personally.

On the other hand, secured lending can sometimes be structured in a way that feels cleaner and more defined than an unsecured facility with a wide personal guarantee attached.

The structure matters more than the headline label.

When Secured Lending Makes More Sense

Secured funding is usually more appropriate when:

  • Borrowing exceeds £100,000
  • Property is involved
  • The funding need is long-term
  • You’re restructuring existing higher-cost debt
  • There’s a clear asset backing the facility

In those cases, avoiding security simply to keep things “unsecured” can end up costing more.

When Unsecured Lending Is the Better Option

Unsecured facilities tend to work well when:

  • The requirement is smaller
  • Speed is critical
  • The need is short-term
  • There isn’t suitable asset security available

For many SMEs, unsecured lending plays a useful role. It just shouldn’t be chosen automatically.

Why Structure Matters More Than Product Type

Going directly to one lender usually means you’re accepting one credit view and one solution.

Working with a commercial finance broker in the UK means comparing:

  • Secured options
  • Unsecured options
  • Cost of capital
  • Director exposure
  • Exit flexibility

At Boxx Commercial Finance, the objective isn’t to push one type of facility.

It’s to structure funding around the outcome the business actually needs.

Sometimes that’s secured. Sometimes it isn’t.

Final Thought

There isn’t a universally “right” answer.

There’s only the structure that supports:

  • Cash flow
  • Growth plans
  • Risk tolerance
  • Cost control

That decision should be made deliberately — not by default.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is a secured business loan always cheaper?

Often, yes — but not always. Security reduces lender risk, which can improve pricing. Overall cost still depends on fees, term length and structure.

Do unsecured business loans require personal guarantees?

In many cases, yes. Even without property security, directors may still provide personal guarantees or debentures.

Can unsecured borrowing be refinanced into secured lending?

Yes. As assets or trading strength improve, refinancing can reduce overall cost of capital.

How much can I borrow unsecured?

It depends on turnover, profitability and credit strength. Unsecured facilities are usually smaller than secured lending.

Speak to a Commercial Finance Specialist

If you’re weighing up secured vs unsecured business loans in the UK, it’s worth structuring it properly from the outset.

content:
If you search “how to get a business loan UK”, most advice focuses on credit scores and filling out forms.

That’s not what usually causes delays.

In reality, funding slows down because:

  • The wrong lender is approached
  • The facility is structured incorrectly
  • The purpose isn’t clearly explained
  • Supporting information is incomplete

Getting funding approved is less about ticking boxes — and more about presenting a commercially sensible case.

Here’s what actually matters.

Step 1: Be Clear on the Purpose of the Funding

Before speaking to any lender, define:

  • How much you need
  • What it will be used for
  • How long you need it
  • How it will be repaid

Buying equipment?
Acquiring property?
Managing cash flow?
Refinancing expensive debt?

Each purpose aligns with a different structure — and often a different lender.

For example:

  • Property purchases may fall under Commercial Mortgages
  • Short-term projects may require Bridging Finance
  • Growth or expansion funding may sit within structured Business Loans

Trying to force one product to fit every scenario is where most businesses lose time.

Step 2: Understand Whether the Loan Should Be Secured or Unsecured

One of the biggest structural decisions is whether the facility should be secured.

Secured facilities involve asset backing and often allow:

  • Larger borrowing amounts
  • Lower pricing
  • Longer terms

Unsecured facilities rely more heavily on trading performance and credit strength.

Both have their place.

If you're unsure, comparing secured and unsecured structures before applying can prevent unnecessary declines or overpricing.

Step 3: Prepare What Lenders Actually Look At

Most lenders will assess:

  • Latest filed accounts
  • Up-to-date management figures
  • Bank statements
  • Existing borrowing
  • Director credit profiles
  • Asset position

What they’re really assessing is risk.

Can the business service the debt?
Is there a sensible exit?
Does the request make commercial sense?

Applications fail more often due to weak presentation than weak businesses.

Step 4: Avoid the “One Lender” Trap

Approaching a single lender directly gives you:

  • One credit appetite
  • One risk model
  • One decision

If that lender says no — or structures it poorly — you’re back to square one.

Working with a commercial finance broker in the UK allows you to:

  • Compare lenders properly
  • Align the facility with the funding purpose
  • Reduce unnecessary credit searches
  • Improve structuring from the outset

That’s usually the difference between a smooth approval and three months of frustration.

Step 5: Structure for the Exit — Not Just the Entry

This is the part most businesses overlook.

It’s not just about getting approved.

It’s about:

  • Can it be refinanced later?
  • Are there early repayment penalties?
  • Does it restrict future borrowing?
  • Is director exposure proportionate?

The cheapest or fastest option isn’t always the best long-term solution.

Funding should support growth — not restrict it.

Common Mistakes When Applying for Business Loans in the UK

Some patterns appear repeatedly:

  • Applying without clarity on purpose
  • Over-borrowing “just in case”
  • Underestimating lender due diligence
  • Ignoring total cost (fees, exit penalties, covenants)
  • Applying to multiple lenders simultaneously

The result is usually delays, declines, or overpaying.

Final Thought

Getting a business loan in the UK isn’t complicated — but it does require structure.

Be clear on the purpose.
Match the right product.
Present it properly.
And think about the exit before signing.

Do that, and approvals become significantly smoother.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does it take to get a business loan in the UK?

It depends on the type of facility. Unsecured loans can complete in days, while secured or property-backed lending may take several weeks.

What credit score do I need for a business loan?

There is no universal minimum. Lenders assess overall risk including trading performance, affordability and security position.

Can I get a business loan as a new company?

Yes, but options may be more limited. Security, director strength and business plan quality become more important.

Is it better to go direct to a lender or use a broker?

Using a commercial finance broker allows comparison of multiple lenders and better structuring, which often improves approval chances and overall terms.

content:
Commercial Mortgage Rates UK – It’s Not Just About the Headline Number

If you're researching commercial mortgage rates in the UK, you’ve probably noticed something quickly:

There isn’t a single rate.

Unlike residential mortgages, commercial lending is priced individually. Two businesses buying similar properties can receive materially different terms.

That’s because lenders assess risk across multiple variables — not just the property itself.

If you’re arranging a commercial mortgage, understanding what actually drives pricing will put you in a stronger position before you apply.

What Determines Commercial Mortgage Rates in the UK?

There are five core factors lenders consider.

1. Loan-to-Value (LTV)

The lower the LTV, the lower the lender’s risk.

A property funded at 60% LTV will generally receive better pricing than one funded at 75%.

Higher leverage increases exposure. Increased exposure increases cost.

This is why deposit size plays a meaningful role in overall pricing — not just approval.

2. Property Type

Not all commercial properties are viewed equally.

Lower-risk examples:

  • Standard office buildings
  • Industrial units
  • Well-located retail

Higher-risk examples:

  • Specialist leisure
  • Care homes
  • Pubs
  • Mixed-use with complex leases

The more niche the property, the more selective lenders become — and the more pricing can widen.

3. Business Strength (Owner-Occupied)

If the property is owner-occupied, lenders assess:

  • Trading history
  • Profitability
  • Cash flow consistency
  • Existing borrowing

A strong business with stable earnings will typically access sharper commercial mortgage rates.

If performance is inconsistent, lenders may either increase pricing or reduce leverage.

4. Rental Coverage (Investment Property)

For investment purchases, rental income becomes central.

Lenders usually require rental cover between:

  • 125% to 145% of interest payments

Stronger lease quality and longer unexpired terms improve risk profile.

Weaker tenant covenant? Expect pricing adjustments.

5. Loan Size and Term

Larger loans often receive stronger pricing because lenders compete more aggressively at scale.

Very small commercial mortgages can carry slightly higher margins.

Loan term also matters.

  • Shorter fixed terms may price differently
  • Longer-term certainty may carry a premium
  • Variable structures offer flexibility but expose to rate movement

Fixed vs Variable Commercial Mortgage Rates

Most lenders offer:

  • Fixed rate options
  • Variable rate options
  • Tracker products

Fixed rates provide certainty, particularly useful in volatile rate environments.

Variable structures may allow greater flexibility, but repayment exposure fluctuates.

There isn’t a universally correct option — it depends on risk appetite and strategy.

How Commercial Mortgage Rates Compare to Other Funding Options

Commercial mortgage pricing is typically:

  • Higher than residential lending
  • Lower than unsecured borrowing
  • Lower than most short-term funding

For short-term property transactions, some businesses initially use Bridging Finance before refinancing onto longer-term commercial mortgages.

For non-property funding needs, structured Business Loans may be more appropriate.

Each product has its place.

The mistake is trying to force one structure to suit every requirement.

Can You Negotiate Commercial Mortgage Rates?

In many cases, yes.

But negotiation doesn’t usually happen in isolation.

It happens through:

  • Strong presentation
  • Correct lender selection
  • Competitive positioning
  • Clear exit strategy
  • Clean credit profile

Submitting a well-structured case via a commercial finance broker in the UK can materially improve pricing outcomes compared to approaching one lender directly.

Lenders price risk. Reduce perceived risk and pricing improves.

What About Fees?

Rate is only part of the equation.

You should also factor in:

  • Arrangement fees
  • Valuation costs
  • Legal fees
  • Exit fees
  • Early repayment penalties

A marginally lower rate with high exit penalties can cost more long term.

Total cost of capital matters more than headline margin.

How to Improve the Rate You’re Offered

While market conditions can’t be controlled, structure can.

Ways to improve pricing include:

  • Increasing deposit
  • Reducing leverage
  • Demonstrating strong management accounts
  • Clearing unnecessary debt before applying
  • Presenting a clear long-term strategy

Sometimes waiting three months to strengthen accounts can improve terms significantly.

Speed and impatience often cost more than businesses realise.

Market Conditions and Rate Environment

Commercial mortgage rates in the UK are influenced by:

  • Bank of England base rate
  • Swap rates
  • Liquidity in the lending market
  • Sector risk appetite

However, lenders do not move in perfect alignment.

Some remain aggressive in certain sectors while others pull back.

Understanding current appetite is often more important than trying to time the base rate.

Final Thought

Commercial mortgage rates are not fixed products on a shelf.

They are risk-priced solutions.

The property matters.
The business matters.
The structure matters.

If you approach the process strategically, pricing reflects that.

If you approach it casually, it often shows in the terms offered.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are average commercial mortgage rates in the UK?

Rates vary widely depending on risk profile, but typically sit above residential rates and below unsecured business lending.

How much deposit do I need for a commercial mortgage?

Most lenders require between 20% and 40%, depending on property type and business strength.

Can I get a fixed-rate commercial mortgage?

Yes. Many lenders offer fixed rate periods ranging from two to ten years.

Do commercial mortgage rates change with the Bank of England base rate?

Variable and tracker products may move with base rate or swap rates, while fixed rates remain stable during the agreed term.
